elements.sent-2411-001: Phishing remains a pervasive cyber threat, as attackers craft deceptive emails to lure victims into revealing sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-002: While Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly deep learning, has become a key component in defending against phishing attacks, these approaches face critical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-003: The scarcity of publicly available, diverse, and updated data, largely due to privacy concerns, constrains their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-004: As phishing tactics evolve rapidly, models trained on limited, outdated data struggle to detect new, sophisticated deception strategies, leaving systems vulnerable to an ever-growing array of attacks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Addressing this gap is essential to strengthening defenses in an increasingly hostile cyber landscape.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address this gap, we propose the Phishing Evolution Network (PEN), a framework leveraging large language models (LLMs) and adversarial training mechanisms to continuously generate high quality and realistic diverse phishing samples, and analyze features of LLM-provided phishing to understand evolving phishing patterns.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate the quality and diversity of phishing samples generated by PEN and find that it produces over 80\% realistic phishing samples, effectively expanding phishing datasets across seven dominant types.
elements.sent-2411-008: These PEN-generated samples enhance the performance of current phishing detectors, leading to a 40\% improvement in detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, the use of PEN significantly boosts model robustness, reducing detectors' sensitivity to perturbations by up to 60\%, thereby decreasing attack success rates under adversarial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-010: When we analyze the phishing patterns that are used in LLM-generated phishing, the cognitive complexity and the tone of time limitation are detected with statistically significant differences compared with existing phishing.

elements.sent-2411-041: To tackle the issues of limited diversity in phishing data and the need for dynamic analysis of phishing tactics, we propose a framework called Phishing Evolution Network (PEN).
elements.sent-2411-042: PEN leverages LLMs to simulate human-crafted phishing emails and utilizes a linguistic tool to examine and track the evolving characteristics of phishing strategies.
elements.sent-2411-043: Initially, the framework uses the existing public datasets to train the TextMorphGAN (TMGAN), a training mechanism leveraging training principles of GAN with LLMs generator and Bi-LSTM detector, for generation.

elements.sent-2411-085: The goals are to i) generate phishing samples with high quality and diversity; ii) understand how different LLMs leverage social engineering features deceiving users compared with existing phishing; iii) identify unique social engineering patterns from the evolution through a reasoning process; and iv) meet the need for a qualitative dataset and the insights into varied SE patterns to improve the current phishing detection mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-086: The PEN consists of four phases, starting with the use of existing phishing datasets and the GAN principle to guide the generator in producing new human-like phishing samples (Section \ref{chapter_pen_A ).}
elements.sent-2411-087: Next, we utilize the state-of-the-art (SOTA) phishing detection models as filters (Section \ref{chapter_pen_B ) to assess the quality of generated phishing samples.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We select the true positive samples as PEN-phishing (i.e., those correctly identified as phishing), which are prepared to analyze the SE phishing patterns.
elements.sent-2411-089: This analysis aims to identify new attacker strategies, the evolution of deceptive techniques, and changing characteristics that may evade detection.
elements.sent-2411-090: We specifically employ unsupervised learning to cluster the samples and then use linguistic tools for fine-grained analysis to uncover evolving phishing patterns (Section \ref{chapter_pen_C ).}
elements.sent-2411-091: The extracted patterns are then fed back into the first phase of the framework, initiating a new iteration (Section \ref{chapter_pen_D ).}
elements.sent-2411-092: Each iteration provides feedback to ensure that critical phishing characteristics are identified and analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-093: The pipeline is designed for generalization and can be updated as new LLMs are released, offering a long-term solution to the evolving phishing landscape.
elements.sent-2411-094: In the first stage of PEN, real-world phishing emails are used to train the generator network (LLM-G) via the adversarial generative network (GAN) principle.
elements.sent-2411-095: The LLM-G and a discriminator (D) compete and refine each other, ultimately enhancing the generator's ability to produce more realistic phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-096: This process, termed TextMorphGAN (TMGAN), is designed to generate content with high quality and diversity, allowing us to observe how output evolves after fine-tuning and revealing shifts in social engineering (SE) tactics.
elements.sent-2411-097: Generator in TextMorphGAN We deploy a fine-tuned Llama 3.1 model with a custom-designed prompt (refer to Figure \ref{fig_llama_prompt_example ), as the generator of TMGAN.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Compared with other LLMs, Llama 3.1 stands out as an open-source model that incorporates the latest advancements in text generation technology.
elements.sent-2411-099: This makes it particularly valuable for generating phishing emails that reflect current trends, ensuring its relevance for research and deceptive tactics development.
elements.sent-2411-100: To fine-tune Llama 3.1 efficiently, we apply the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) technique \cite{hu2021lora , which is well-suited for large pre-trained models with limited computational resources.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The LLM generator leverages latent representations to simulate features from real text data and provides samples that closely mimic real-world phishing content.
elements.sent-2411-102: Through adversarial training, the discriminator differentiates between real and generated content while providing feedback to the generator.
elements.sent-2411-103: We conducted an ablation study to compare the performance of Llama 3.1 generators with that of different states in phishing email generation.
elements.sent-2411-104: Detailed results and methodology can be found in Appendix \ref{appendix:chapter_prompts_ablation_study , where we discuss our selection of the optimal generator model.}
elements.sent-2411-106: After determining the generator, we assess the realism of the generated samples.
elements.sent-2411-107: For this task, we employ a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network, an enhanced version of the recurrent neural network (RNN).
elements.sent-2411-108: Bi-LSTM features input, forget, and output gates to effectively handle long-term dependencies in one-directional text analysis.
elements.sent-2411-109: By capturing both past and future content features without overlap, Bi-LSTM excels in classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-110: Recent advances have shown that pre-trained LLMs can also achieve high accuracy in text classification \cite{uddin2024explainable .}
elements.sent-2411-111: However, given the extensive number of parameters in LLMs, using them as a discriminator in TMGAN would be computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-112: Balancing execution efficiency and detection accuracy, we ultimately chose Bi-LSTM as the discriminator for TMGAN.
elements.sent-2411-114: To effectively guide the TMGAN, we avoid directly inputting the filtered phishing samples into generation models.
elements.sent-2411-115: Instead, we designed a structured chat prompt template inspired by the Chain Of Thought (CoT \cite{wei2022chain ).}
elements.sent-2411-116: This template consists of three key sections: Instructions, Topic Keywords, and Phishing Emails (Figure \ref{fig_llama_prompt_example ).}
elements.sent-2411-117: Each section is designed to incrementally guide the content generation process as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-118: Instructions: This section sets the context by defining the model as a cybersecurity expert, helping to minimize hallucinations and focus the model’s responses on specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-119: Notably, including an email label here is essential for specifying the type of email (e.g., phishing or legitimate).
elements.sent-2411-120: Without this label, even if phishing-related keywords are present, the model might still generate content that resembles legitimate emails.
elements.sent-2411-121: Topic Keywords: These keywords act as guiding constraints, directing the model’s creativity while ensuring coherence with the desired themes or topics.
elements.sent-2411-122: This approach enhances topic consistency, enabling the generation of diverse content that remains relevant to the phishing theme.
elements.sent-2411-123: Phishing Emails (Existing Data): This section provides examples of both phishing and legitimate emails, serving as output templates.
elements.sent-2411-124: These examples help clarify the expected response format, guiding the model in interpreting the given instructions and generating plausible content.
elements.sent-2411-125: The design incorporates a one-to-many relationship between the output templates and topic keywords, preventing limitations in the diversity of the generated PEN content.
elements.sent-2411-126: As outlined in Section \ref{chapter_pen_A , the generator is then fine-tuned using the comprehensive prompt in TMGAN, triggering a new iteration of the process.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In this step, we obtain an initial generated dataset for the framework, labeled as PEN-generated phishing.

elements.sent-2411-128: The goal of the second stage is to validate the realism of the generated results (RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-129: Once we obtain the PEN-generated phishing samples, we use commercial phishing detection tools to filter and retain those classified as ``phishing'', which we denote as PEN-phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-130: Additionally, each PEN-phishing email was further annotated using widely recognized social engineering (SE) psychological and persuasion principles.
elements.sent-2411-131: This step helps to eliminate outliers (i.e., samples classified as phishing by the commercial tool but lacking any commonly identified SE principles).
elements.sent-2411-132: We refer to the final output as PEN-phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-133: This approach provides a set of high-quality, human-like phishing samples for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-134: Specifically, we fine-tuned the IBM Nature Language Model (NLU) and Llama 3.1 classifier for phishing email classification.
elements.sent-2411-135: Unlike standard state-of-the-art methods (e.g., Gmail and Outlook filters), our models focus on deeper contextual phishing detection, particularly targeting the email body discovery.
elements.sent-2411-136: IBM NLU, part of IBM Cloud \cite{IBM-Cloud , offers pre-trained models that can be fine-tuned for specific tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Using the F1-score as our evaluation metric, the fine-tuned IBM NLU and Llama 3.1 classifiers achieved performance scores of 98.7\% and 99.8\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-138: To understand the diverse SE strategies and characteristics of the filtered phishing samples (RQ2), we first cluster filtered samples into several interpretable categories.
elements.sent-2411-139: This is followed by established persuasion principles annotation and word level language features analysis.
elements.sent-2411-140: Specifically, we applied Latent Dirichlet Analysis (LDA) for text clustering, as it can automatically discover topics without requiring predefined categories \cite{blei2003latent .}
elements.sent-2411-141: The optimal number of topics of clusters is determined by the text coherence (range from 0 to 1, with higher scores indicating stronger topic coherence).
elements.sent-2411-142: The LDA-generated topic keywords play a key role in guiding the generation of new phishing samples in subsequent iterations, ensuring contextually coherent and thematically consistent content (see Figure \ref{fig_llama_prompt_example ).}
elements.sent-2411-143: Next, we delve into the SE characteristics of each cluster, focusing on Cialdini's persuasion principles \cite{cialdini2001science : Authority, Reciprocity, Scarcity, Liking, and Social Proof.}
elements.sent-2411-144: These principles are commonly leveraged by attackers to shape human behavior and influence decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-145: For instance, attackers might employ these tactics to create a sense of urgency, establish trust, or instill a sense of obligation in their targets.
elements.sent-2411-146: The SE features are crafted to exploit human vulnerabilities, making these phishing attacks particularly effective and dangerous \cite{wang2021social .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Our goal is to compare the usage of persuasion principles in traditional phishing with those generated by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-148: We used the LLooM framework for principle annotation, an integrated tool that distills input, clusters data, and outputs summarized concepts \cite{lam2024concept .}
elements.sent-2411-149: By inputting the persuasion principles along with their definitions, LLooM performs inverse annotation to identify the employed principles among the filtered phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-150: To gain deeper insights into specific principles (e.g., differences in word usage and language style between two phishing datasets), we employed Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) \cite{boyd2022development .}
elements.sent-2411-151: LIWC quantifies linguistic features using a pre-constructed dictionary that associates words with psychological insights, covering emotional, social, cognitive, and authority-based language \cite{boyd2022development .}
elements.sent-2411-152: These insights align with psychologically meaningful word groups, each linked to one or more persuasion principles.
elements.sent-2411-153: For example, the principle of Social Proof reflects human tendencies toward conformity and peer influence, where terms like ``everyone'' and ``most people'' are often relevant indicators.
elements.sent-2411-154: By leveraging these tools, we aim to: 1) uncover deceptive characteristics through psychological and semantic analysis; and 2) illustrate the evolution of phishing techniques, including variations in the use of persuasion principles, word choice, communication style, and emotional expression.
elements.subsection-2411-006: D. Loop-back Phishing Samples with New Patterns
elements.sent-2411-155: Our approach creates a closed-loop framework by reintegrating high-quality PEN-phishing samples back into the training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-156: In this phase, we insert extracted features of PEN-phishing (e.g., topic keywords, labels, and email content) into the prompts used during training, starting a new cycle of training, generation, and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-157: This iterative loop enables the continuous refinement of the phishing sample dataset, integrating evolving patterns and themes while maintaining high-quality, diverse content generation aligned with current phishing tactics.
elements.sent-2411-158: In the first phase of our PEN, we train the generator (LLM-G, or $G$ for short) and discriminator (D) through the adversarial training (via the GAN principle).
elements.sent-2411-159: This competitive process mutually improves their performance, ultimately enhancing the generator's generative capability \cite{goodfellow2014generative .}
elements.sent-2411-160: To be specific, the generator $G(\cdot)$ that learns to capture the data distribution while the discriminator $D(\cdot)$ that focuses on estimating the probability that a sample came from the training data in place of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-161: The generator $G$ learns a generator distribution $p_g$ used for generating realistic phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-162: To do that, it builds a mapping function from a prior distribution $p_z$, conditioned on a template prompt, to the data space $G(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-163: In the meantime, the discriminator $D$ outputs a scalar representing the probability that $\bm{x}$ came from training data (i.e., $p_{data}$) rather than $p_g$ \cite{goodfellow2014generative .}
elements.sent-2411-164: As mentioned in Section \ref{chapter_pen_D , a template prompt has been designed to optimize $G(\cdot)$ net performance.}
elements.sent-2411-165: As the training data consists of benign and phishing content, we leverage label $y$ as assistant information and embed it into Instructions of prompts, clarifying and guiding generations.
elements.sent-2411-166: To be more specific, we use the label $y$ (of a specific data $\bm{x}$) by embedding its information (i.e., phishing or benign) into a corresponding prompt.
elements.sent-2411-167: We denote the prompt as $I_{g}$, which serves as the input to the $G(\cdot)$.
elements.sent-2411-168: With the label class information, $G(\cdot)$ net is guided to generate more targeted samples.
elements.sent-2411-169: In our proposed framework, during this training process, the generator $G$ and the discriminator $D$ are trained simultaneously via the following objective function: \begin{equation}\min_{G}\max_{D} \ \ E_{\bm{x}\sim p_{data}}[logD(\bm{x})]+E_{\bm{z}\sim p_{z}}[log(1-D(G(I_{g})))]\label{eq:v1}\end{equation} In the objective function shown in Eq.(\ref{eq:v1 ), the goal of $G(\cdot)$ net is to generate text that resembles real data and corresponds to label $y$ (i.e. if label $y$ is phishing, the generator $G(\cdot)$ is trained to generate real phishing data).}
elements.sent-2411-170: In particular, the generator minimizes its loss by updating its parameters to generate more realistic data, and the discriminator minimizes its loss by updating its parameters to become better at distinguishing real and generated data.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the end, after the training phase, we get the generator $G$ with applicability on label-instruction text generation missions.
elements.sent-2411-172: The above networks, $G(\cdot)$ and $D(\cdot)$ are often non-linear mapping functions \cite{mirza2014conditional , and in our framework, we employ Llama 3.1 8B \cite{Llama-3.1 in $G$ net.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Simultaneously harness Bi-LISM Attention as $D$ net.

elements.sent-2411-326: The iterative framework is essential for mitigating the problem of information lag caused by the evolving nature of phishing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-327: By continuously updating datasets and predictive models, the framework adapts to emerging threats, aligning with the changing tactics of attackers.
elements.sent-2411-328: PEN is designed as a closed-loop system that simulates phishing attacks, generates framework-specific phishing datasets, and analyzes potential phishing features through these simulations.
elements.sent-2411-329: In previous sections, we demonstrated the effectiveness of PEN, with Llama 3.1 serving as a core model.
elements.sent-2411-330: Comparative analysis of various generators (Appendix \ref{appendix:chapter_prompts_ablation_study ) confirmed Llama 3.1's superiority in generating phishing content, showing a better simulation of human writing styles (Section \ref{chapter_RQ1 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-331: Additionally, Llama 3.1 demonstrated greater adaptability and robustness across to different input data and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-332: Using the PEN-phishing dataset generated by Llama 3.1, we enhanced the detection system's robustness, reducing its sensitivity to perturbation attacks and significantly improving detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-333: We employed two types of detectors for phishing detection, focusing specifically on minimizing sensitivity to adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-334: Initial tests used a dataset of existing emails (phishing and benign), followed by evaluations against a set of 500 emails designed for perturbation attacks.
elements.sent-2411-335: After this baseline testing, we fine-tune the detectors using filtered PEN-phishing samples and subjected them to a second round of perturbation attacks.
elements.sent-2411-336: We used metrics like accuracy, F1-score, and attack successfully rate (ASR) to assess the effectiveness of the generated data.
elements.sent-2411-337: The detectors based on models ALBERT, RoBERTa, and SQ were initially trained to classify human-written phishing versus benign emails, which we denote as $D_{1}$.
elements.sent-2411-338: Meanwhile, DistilBERT, RoBERTa-LLMs, and T5-Encoder form the $D_{2}$ group, tasked with distinguishing between LLM-generated phishing and human-written phishing.
elements.sent-2411-339: The adversarial attacks considered include character-level manipulations such as swaps, substitutions, deletions, and insertions of significant words.
elements.sent-2411-340: Attack tools used were TextFooler \cite{wang2020infobert , PWWS \cite{ren2019generating , Pruthi \cite{pruthi2019combating and DeepWordBug \cite{gao2018black .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Table \ref{tab:deepwordbug_attack_before and \ref{tab:deepwordbug_attack_after present model performance before and after fine-tuning with PEN-phishing data.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Initial results showed that detectors struggled with phishing detection (Table \ref{tab:deepwordbug_attack_before ).}
elements.sent-2411-343: The best-performing model, RoBERTa in $D_{1}$, achieved only 0.54 accuracy and 0.57 F1-score, and was highly vulnerable to perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-344: In $D_{2}$, T5-Encoder performed best before fine-tuning, with 0.53 accuracy, 0.68 F1-score, and a lower ASR of 6.74\%.
elements.sent-2411-345: However, after fine-tuning with PEN-phishing (Table \ref{tab:deepwordbug_attack_after ), we observed a substantial improvement in robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-346: For $D_{1}$, ALBERT's ASR dropped from 67.15\% to 40.91\%, while RoBERTa's ASR decreased to 8.76\%.
elements.sent-2411-347: In $D_{2}$, all models achieved near-perfect detection accuracy and F1-score (both are 0.99) and a strong defense against attacks, with RoBERTa-Syn successfully countering 63\% of perturbation attempts.
elements.sent-2411-348: The marked decrease in ASR after fine-tuning highlights the value of PEN-generated phishing data in improving the robustness of detectors, particularly against evasion attacks.
elements.sent-2411-349: Further details on the types of attacks and fine-tuning parameters are provided in Appendix \ref{appendix:evasion_attacks_other_results .}
elements.sent-2411-350: Additionally, this approach improves performance in non-attack scenarios, with F1-scores increasing by at least 30\%.. \end{tcolorbox.}
elements.sent-2411-351: We now discuss the limitations and suggest future work.
elements.sent-2411-353: According to the datasets, PEN indeed with the ability of benign email generation.
elements.sent-2411-354: In this work, we concentrate on phishing email generation, verification, and analysis, while overlooking the legitimate content from PEN.
elements.sent-2411-355: Still, generating these legitimate emails is critical to meet broader user needs \cite{zhang2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-356: Future work about benign content from PEN can focus on enhancing the relevance of generated content to requirements and ensuring it conveys meaningful information for practical use.
elements.sent-2411-358: To study changes in social engineering tactics, we used LLooM to label persuasion principles in PEN-phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-359: Our analysis shows that most LLM-driven phishing samples include at least persuasion principles.
elements.sent-2411-360: However, some samples, that lacked any of the six labeled principles (outlier), were overlooked, even though they were classified as true positive phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-361: Future research can explore if any new human vulnerabilities are used in outliers to understand more complex social engineering tactics.
elements.sent-2411-363: The goal of PEN is to analyze shifts in social engineering tactics in LLM-generated phishing emails, though the framework can also apply to text-based attacks created by jailbreak techniques.
elements.sent-2411-364: LLMs face various attacks, including Prompt Injections, Jailbreaks, and System Message Extraction \cite{yi2024jailbreak .}
elements.sent-2411-365: Although OpenAI has introduced layered instruction mechanisms to reduce such attacks, some legitimate-looking requests can still hide malicious intentions and bypass detection \cite{wallace2024instruction .}
elements.sent-2411-366: For example, we evade detection by asking the model to create phishing emails using specific keywords according to the provided output, which misleads the LLM into generating harmful output.
elements.sent-2411-367: Our work shows the importance of defending against attacks like prompt jailbreaking, with PEN’s design enabling analysis of key attack features.. Multi-language Phishing Analysing.
elements.sent-2411-368: By utilizing the training data and generative capabilities of LLMs, our framework can be adapted for multilingual research on phishing psychology.
elements.sent-2411-369: In our experiments, the model generated content in both Chinese and Japanese, whether phishing strategies vary across different languages is a direction for future research.
elements.sent-2411-370: Recent Advancements in Improving Phishing Data Quality.
elements.sent-2411-371: To enhance datasets, studies like \cite{al2021generating,seymour2016weaponizing combine Feature Engineering and Deep Learning (DL) methods.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Common attributes, such as function words like ``click'' and ``urgent'', are extracted and used to fine-tune DL models, helping generate more phishing content.
elements.sent-2411-373: However, FE faces challenges 1) the extracted features may not fully represent phishing strategies, and 2) it may fail to capture the latest tactics used by cybercriminals \cite{hitaj2019passgan .}
elements.sent-2411-374: To address these issues, data augmentation has emerged as a promising alternative \cite{regina2020text .}
elements.sent-2411-375: For example, Gholampour et al. generated phishing emails using text augmenters like Textfooler \cite{jin2020bert , which introduces slight text variations by adding, replacing, or removing words while preserving meaning, thus increasing dataset diversity \cite{mehdi2023adversarial .}}
elements.sent-2411-376: However, augmented data may still not fully capture real-world attack patterns and cannot always be verified for authenticity \cite{li2023spear .}
elements.sent-2411-377: The problem of insufficient representativeness of phishing features has become more prominent due to the development of large language models (LLMs) as limited knowledge of new ways phishing deceives victims, LLM-generated malicious content for example.
elements.sent-2411-378: Many studies revealed that LLMs crafted phishing has been more targeted, individualized, and effective in tricking the intended audience \cite{toolan2010feature .}
elements.sent-2411-379: A recent study in \cite{bethany2024large analyzing LLM-generated phishing datasets collected from 9,000 individuals during 11 months indicates that around 80\% of the targeted 208 individuals opened the email, and 36.05\% of them followed the instructions by clicking the provided link, worse is that approximately a quarter of participants provided attackers required sensitive data.}
elements.sent-2411-380: Roy et al. proposed a closed framework that auto-generates the phishing scams according to LLMs self-produced prompts \cite{roy2024chatbots .}
elements.sent-2411-381: Key findings from their research are that LLMs can both provide malicious webpages and phishing emails that convincingly simulate trust brands and other tactics that are used to evade current anti-phishing detection mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-382: Even worse, they found that LLM-driven attacks can also be successfully implemented without the need for red flag prompts.
elements.sent-2411-383: The emergence of LLMs accelerates the rapid development of phishing email attacks, where countermeasures of FE and data augmentation, which focus on attributes such as semantics and characteristics, can hard fully represent the newly emerging phishing strategies, making them unsuitable for the current evolving landscape of phishing deceptive development.
elements.sent-2411-384: Our work focuses on addressing the research gaps related to the accessibility and usability of existing phishing datasets, as well as understanding the newly emerging phishing features that have yet to be fully captured in current models.
elements.sent-2411-385: Phishing Tactics from Psychological and Machine-Generated Deception.
elements.sent-2411-386: Phishing attacks using hyperlinks on web pages are common.
elements.sent-2411-387: A study analyzed client-side cloaking techniques on phishing sites, identifying eight effective evasion methods, such as user interaction, to bypass detection \cite{zhang2021crawlphish .}
elements.sent-2411-388: Later, Lee et al. categorized user interaction into three distinct methods and conducted a deeper analysis of script-level evasion tactics \cite{lee2024beneath .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Deep learning (DL) has been used to capture semantic features in phishing text datasets, consistently improving detection accuracy beyond traditional static webpage analysis.
elements.sent-2411-390: For instance, Uddin and Sarker proposed DistilBERT detect phishing features, addressing class imbalance and using explainable AI to clarify prediction results \cite{uddin2024explainable .}
elements.sent-2411-391: Machine learning methods also leverage semantic analysis to strengthen phishing detectors \cite{bagui2019classifying,nguyen2024pioneering .}
elements.sent-2411-392: While detection accuracy has improved, the interpretability of model-extracted features remains a challenge for human understanding.
elements.sent-2411-393: Phishing features explained from psychological social engineering tactics\footnote{Social Engineering in Cybersecurity (SEiCS) has been defined as an attack method where attackers exploit human psychological vulnerabilities through social interactions to compromise cybersecurity.}
elements.sent-2411-394: Human vulnerabilities involve cognition and knowledge, behavior and habit, emotions and feelings, human nature, personality traits, and six aspects of individual character.
elements.sent-2411-395: The interaction enables human manipulation tactics, sending phishing emails for example, which can exploit victims' psychological tendencies to more readily trust authority \cite{wang2020defining .}
elements.sent-2411-396: The interaction between high extroversion and the persuasion principles was confirmed to increase the susceptibility to phishing, especially principles of ``liking'', ``authority and commitment/consistency'', ``authority and liking'' \cite{lawson2020email .}
elements.sent-2411-397: Additionally, participants will label an email as a phishing attempt if they found ``authority'' and ``scarcity'' corresponding content in it.
elements.sent-2411-398: Accordingly, later works combine the persuasive principles into machine learning to predict the successful rate of phishing attacks \cite{li2020detection .}
elements.sent-2411-399: Van Der Heijden and Allodi quantitatived the principles in phishing emails, found that more cognitive weaknesses are leveraged by attackers, higher probability are deceived \cite{van2019cognitive .}
elements.sent-2411-400: Opposite to their finding, there is no evidence to prove principles of ``Social Proof'' and ``Liking'' have a significant effect on the final decision, whereas ``Authority'' have a positive influence effect even given a few non-zero data points.
elements.sent-2411-401: Earlier research has primarily concentrated on the psychological dynamics of phishing in human-to-human (H2H) interactions, however, with the emergence of LLMs, the interaction pattern has shifted to machine-human interactions \cite{wang2020defining, Quan24email .}
elements.sent-2411-402: This transition has fueled the rapid development of phishing attacks and led to the emergence of distinct characteristics \cite{schmitt2023digital .}
elements.sent-2411-403: Therefore, the features derived from analyzing the traditional H2H interaction model may not fully explain the characteristics of the content generated by LLMs \cite{salahdine2019social .}
elements.sent-2411-404: This is another issue addressed in our study.
elements.sent-2411-405: Phishing continues to be a widespread cyber threat, with attackers using deceptive emails to steal sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-406: Although AI, especially deep learning, plays a critical role in defending against phishing, the lack of diverse, up-to-date data due to privacy concerns limits its effectiveness, making it difficult for models to detect new and sophisticated phishing tactics.
elements.sent-2411-407: We propose the Phishing Evolution Network (PEN) framework, which leverages LLMs to simulate phishing generation.
elements.sent-2411-408: It is an iterative framework that enables us to generate and track the evolving tactics of LLM-generated phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-409: The framework is capable of producing high-quality, diverse phishing samples, which also enhances the robustness of current detectors against adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-410: Additionally, we incorporate social engineering and linguistic analysis to gain a deeper understanding of the differences between LLM-generated phishing and existing phishing datasets.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \begin{appendices.}}

elements.sent-2411-412: Specific acquisition methods can be found through relevant references in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-413: In total, we collected six phishing datasets and one benign email dataset.
elements.sent-2411-414: The emails span a broad timestamp, covering publicly available datasets from 1998 to 2021.
elements.sent-2411-415: It is noteworthy that an email might appear multiple times, as different sources employ varying storage practices.
elements.sent-2411-416: Data pre-processing was essential, including deduplication, adjusting email lengths, removing highly similar emails, and standardizing storage formats.

elements.sent-2411-466: While Artificial Intelligence (AI), in particular, deep learning, has become a key component in defending against phishing attacks, these approaches face critical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-467: The scarcity of publicly available, diverse, and updated data, largely due to privacy concerns, constrains detection effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-468: As phishing tactics evolve rapidly, models trained on limited, outdated data struggle to detect new, sophisticated deception strategies, leaving systems and people vulnerable to an ever-growing array of attacks.
elements.sent-2411-469: We propose the first Phishing Evolution FramEworK (PEEK) for augmenting phishing email datasets with respect to quality and diversity, and analyzing changing phishing patterns for detection to adapt to updated phishing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-470: Specifically, we integrate large language models (LLMs) into the process of adversarial training to enhance the performance of the generated dataset and leverage persuasion principles in a recurrent framework to facilitate the understanding of changing phishing strategies.
elements.sent-2411-471: PEEK raises the proportion of usable phishing samples from 21.4\% to 84.8\%, surpassing existing works that rely on prompting and fine-tuning LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-472: The phishing datasets provided by PEEK, with evolving phishing patterns, outperform the other two available LLM-generated phishing email datasets in improving detection robustness.
elements.sent-2411-473: PEEK phishing boosts detectors' accuracy to over 88\% and reduces adversarial sensitivity by up to 70\%, still maintaining 70\% detection accuracy against adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-474: Email phishing is one of the most common attacks in phishing campaigns, which poses threats to individuals and organizations globally \cite{gupta2017fighting .}
elements.sent-2411-475: In the fourth quarter of 2024, losses attributed to reported phishing email attacks exceeded \$3.6 billion worldwide, nearly double the third quarter \cite{APWG_phish_report , highlighting the significance of detecting phishing emails.}
elements.sent-2411-476: Currently, deep learning (DL) \cite{salloum2022systematic,alhogail2021applying and large language models (LLMs) \cite{koide2024chatspamdetector,chen2024survey have been identified as the most powerful phishing detection approaches.}}
elements.sent-2411-477: Their performance is highly dependent on the characteristics of the available data, as these models identify phishing email campaigns based on the features learned from the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-478: Ideally, the datasets for training detectors should be of high quality to be realistic, diverse across domains, and represent up-to-date phishing attacks to improve the robustness of detectors \cite{he2020towards .}
elements.sent-2411-479: Yet, challenges remain in collecting phishing emails with high quality and diversity, not least due to privacy concerns.
elements.sent-2411-480: Phishing emails that are reported to vendors are not shared publicly to protect users' private information from leaking.
elements.sent-2411-481: Additionally, phishing strategies change dynamically, while delays in the updating of phishing attacks result in detector training based on outdated datasets and a limited range of phishing subjects \cite{iwaya2023privacy,das2019sok .}
elements.sent-2411-482: To solve the limitations, researchers have made efforts to expand available phishing datasets by deploying DL models in conjunction with feature engineering (FE) \cite{salloum2021phishing and data augmentation techniques \cite{regina2020text,shirazi2019adversarial,alkadri2022enhancing .}}
elements.sent-2411-483: Despite promising results, FE-based methods still insufficiently capture phishing features \cite{das2019sok and therefore achieve limited coverage of diverse phishing strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-484: Concerning data augmentation, automatic methods such as adversarial training~\cite{tang2023data,xu2022adversarial outperform rule-based approaches \cite{zhou2022rule in reducing subjective intervention.}}
elements.sent-2411-485: Yet, the augmented datasets exhibit a diversity bias that hinders their effectiveness~\cite{chen2023empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-486: Recently, LLMs have been utilized to generate phishing datasets due to their advanced generation capability.
elements.sent-2411-487: The IWSPA\_2023 phishing datasets \cite{mehdi2023adversarial were provided through a fine-tuned GPT-2 that employs perturbation tools, aiming to defend against phishing threats.}
elements.sent-2411-488: Nevertheless, the phishing emails provided by the method rely heavily on available datasets and with the risk of noisy outputs \cite{alomar2023data , hindering the progress in phishing quality and diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-489: Another phishing email dataset is synthesized by DeepAI, an LLM-based generation tool, through prompts with phishing attempts and available Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) \cite{eze2024analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-490: However, DeepAI-phishing lacks an assessment of phishing capability, which raises questions about whether realistic phishing patterns are preserved \cite{das2019sok , potentially impairing the effectiveness of detectors due to inadequate feature learning.}
elements.sent-2411-491: Although LLMs have considerable potential for generating phishing emails \cite{zieni2023phishing , systematic methodologies for evaluating LLM-generated phishing quality and enhancing diversity remain underdeveloped.}
elements.sent-2411-492: Additionally, a subsequent challenge lies in understanding whether and how phishing patterns exploited by LLM-generated phishing diverge from those found in existing phishing, which is critical for comprehending unseen phishing threats.
elements.sent-2411-493: Furthermore, the question remains as to whether and how detectors can effectively improve robustness against changing phishing patterns and adapt to changing phishing tactics if training data does not reflect this dynamicity.
elements.sent-2411-494: To tackle the aforementioned issues, we propose PEEK, an innovative Phishing Evolution Framework that leverages an adversarial training process to enhance the quality of synthetically generated phishing data, diversify phishing topics, and provide a long-term solution for interpreting changing phishing patterns.
elements.sent-2411-495: To solve the challenge of limited phishing datasets, we employed and trained one of the state-of-the-art (SOTA) generative LLMs, Llama 3.1 8B, and integrated it within a generative adversarial network (GAN), which consists of a generator providing phishing content and a discriminator distinguishing between given and realistic phishing content.
elements.sent-2411-496: We leveraged the adversarial training process to enhance the generation capability and refine the quality of the provided phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-497: The generated set is fed into an LLM-based analyzer for phishing validation, and we retain samples that are labeled as phishing.
elements.sent-2411-498: To comprehend new emerging patterns, we identify differences and cluster into groups to reveal distinctions in deceptive strategies from initial training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-499: Results (i.e., evolving phishing topic keywords) are incorporated into crafted chat prompts to strengthen diversity; these prompts are then fed back for further iterations, building PEEK as a recurrent framework that is executed iteratively.
elements.sent-2411-500: We ran two iterations of the PEEK framework, which enables large-scale phishing-sample generation and downstream analysis while minimising overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-501: The resulting PEEK phishing corpus and accompanying models will be released publicly upon the acceptance of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-502: We evaluated the utility of PEEK phishing based on several criteria, including quality, diversity, and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-503: Quality assessment is based on two types of metrics: 1) To quantify the model's generation capability, LLM generation coherence (LLM-GC) and perplexity (PPL) are used, and scores of 0.83 and 6.95, respectively, indicate that the generator is effective in generating logically coherent and contextually reliable phishing samples.

elements.sent-2411-505: For diversity, we employed isolation forest score (IFS) and deceptive persuasion score (DPS) to identify anomalous phishing patterns and quantify persuasion strategies.
elements.sent-2411-506: The IFS revealed a statistically significant difference in features, and differences in DPS suggest that deception strategies in PEEK phishing differ from those in existing phishing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-507: For instance, PEEK phishing demonstrates varied vocabulary that reduces urgency, potentially decreasing recipients' alertness to the phishing intent.
elements.sent-2411-508: Regarding robustness, we evaluated PEEK phishing effectiveness by assessing the impact on detector robustness, using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, and attack success rate (ASR).
elements.sent-2411-509: The results show a significant reduction in ASR, with detectors resisting up to 99.2\% while maintaining an F1-score above 0.88.
elements.sent-2411-510: Furthermore, our experiments in various phishing topics generation achieved a PAS analysis of 88.1\%, demonstrating the extensibility and adaptability of PEEK.
elements.sent-2411-511: We propose the Phishing Evolution Framework (PEEK), leveraging adversarial training to enhance and expand the utility of phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-512: The iterative framework allows for generating and tracing the continuously changing tactics of LLM-generated phishing samples.
elements.sent-2411-513: We perform a comprehensive analysis with the framework based on seven real-world datasets, uncovering key characteristics of LLM-driven phishing generation.
elements.sent-2411-514: Our framework is capable of generating phishing samples that exhibit both high quality and increased diversity, thereby improving the robustness of detectors against adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-515: We incorporate persuasion principles and linguistic analysis to deeply understand the difference between LLM-phishing and existing phishing datasets.
elements.sent-2411-516: Additionally, the current extracted phishing features are unsuitable for explaining dynamically changing LLM-phishing strategies.

elements.sent-2411-519: We aim to answer the following questions: RQ1: How to leverage LLMs to enhance the quality and diversity of phishing emails based on available phishing datasets? Enhancing the quality and diversity of phishing samples is crucial for improving the robustness and generalization of phishing detectors.
elements.sent-2411-520: Recently, motivated by the extensive general language training corpora, LLMs have been broadly applied to augment phishing datasets.

elements.sent-2411-523: We propose a novel pipeline that leverages adversarial principles to enhance the realism of LLM-phishing emails and employs chat-based prompt generation to diversify attack scenarios within ethical usage.
elements.sent-2411-524: RQ2: What are the differences in persuasive phishing strategies between existing and LLM-generated phishing datasets? elements.sent-2411-525: These results can provide insights into changing attack strategies.
elements.sent-2411-526: Analyzing synthetic phishing data can benefit from recent work using LLM-driven phishing email detection for phishing intention detection \cite{heiding2023devising,bethany2024large .}

elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth, clip, trim=0.3cm 0.3cm 0.3cm 0.3cm ]{Visualization/PEEK.png} \caption{Structure, phases and steps of the Phishing Evolution Framework (PEEK). elements.sent-2411-534: Fig.~\ref{fig_pen presents an overview of our proposed Phishing Evolution Framework (PEEK).}
elements.sent-2411-535: The PEEK consists of four phases, starting with the use of existing phishing datasets and the GAN principle to guide the generator in producing new human-like phishing samples (Section \ref{chapter_pen_A ).}
elements.sent-2411-536: Next, we utilize a state-of-the-art phishing analyzer to assess the quality of generated phishing samples (Section \ref{chapter_pen_B ).}
elements.sent-2411-537: We select the samples that are identified as phishing, referred to as PEEK phishing, which are subsequently prepared to identify new attacker strategies and changing characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-538: We specifically employ an anomaly-identifying algorithm to find differences with statistical significance, followed by unsupervised clustering and a linguistic tool for lexical level analysis to uncover evolving phishing patterns (Section \ref{chapter_pen_C ).}
elements.sent-2411-539: In the first stage of PEEK, real-world phishing emails are used to train the generator network (LLM-G) via the adversarial generative network (GAN) principle.
elements.sent-2411-540: The complete training procedure is outlined in Appendix~\ref{appendix:object_function .}
elements.sent-2411-541: This process, termed GAN-based training, is designed to generate content with high quality and diversity, allowing us to observe how output evolves after fine-tuning and revealing shifts in social engineering tactics.. Generator Network.
elements.sent-2411-542: We deploy a Llama 3.1 8B model (referred to as Llama 3.1 in the paper) with a custom-designed prompt (refer to Fig.~\ref{fig_llama_prompt_example ), as the generator of GAN.}

elements.sent-2411-544: We conducted an ablation study to compare the performance of different generators (e.g., GPT-2) with Llama 3.1 in phishing generation with that of different states (pre-trained and fine-tuned).
elements.sent-2411-545: The methodology, configuration, and results can be found in Appendix \ref{appendix:chapter_prompts_ablation_study .. Discriminator Network.}
elements.sent-2411-546: For this task, we employ a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network \cite{jang2020bi to assess the realism of the generated samples from the generator.}
elements.sent-2411-547: Bi-LSTM is an enhanced version of the recurrent neural network (RNN), incorporating input, forget, and output gates to effectively model long-term dependencies in sequences.
elements.sent-2411-548: Unlike standard LSTMs, Bi-LSTMs process input sequences in both forward and backward directions, allowing the model to capture contextual information from both preceding and succeeding tokens.
elements.sent-2411-549: This bidirectional context significantly enhances performance in text classification tasks, particularly when the meaning of a token depends on its surrounding context.
elements.sent-2411-550: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance in text classification \cite{uddin2024explainable , employing LLMs as a discriminator poses a risk of classification bias induced by prompting-specific preference, and their substantial parameter size introduces significant computational overhead in adversarial training.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Thus, to balance execution efficiency and detection accuracy, we selected a Bi-LSTM as the discriminator.
elements.sent-2411-553: To effectively guide the generation during GAN training, we avoid directly inputting the filtered phishing samples into the generation models.
elements.sent-2411-554: Instead, we designed a structured chat prompt template inspired by the Chain of Thought (CoT \cite{wei2022chain ).}
elements.sent-2411-555: This template consists of three key sections: Instructions, Topics, and Phishing Emails~(Fig.~\ref{fig_llama_prompt_example ).}
elements.sent-2411-556: Without this label, the model might still generate content resembling legitimate emails, even if phishing-related keywords exist.
elements.sent-2411-557: Topics: A list of keywords serves as guiding constraints, directing the model’s creativity while ensuring coherence with the desired themes or topics.
elements.sent-2411-558: These examples help clarify the expected response format, guiding the model to interpret the given instructions and generate plausible content.
elements.sent-2411-559: The design incorporates a one-to-many relationship between the output templates and topics, preventing limitations in the diversity of the generated PEEK content.
elements.sent-2411-560: As outlined in Section \ref{chapter_pen_A , the generator is then fine-tuned using the comprehensive prompt in GAN, triggering a new iteration of the process.}
elements.sent-2411-561: In this step, we obtain an initial generated dataset for the framework, labeled as PEEK-generated phishing samples.

elements.sent-2411-562: We deploy ChatGPT as an analyzer to validate and retain PEEK-generated phishing samples that are classified as ``phishing'' based on the prompt designed by Koide \cite{koide2024chatspamdetector .}
elements.sent-2411-563: The prompt performs a thorough analysis of phishing attempts and quantifies phishing determination.
elements.sent-2411-564: Phishing samples after this step are labeled as PEEK phishing.
elements.sent-2411-565: The analyzer undergoes a reliability test using cross-validation and is assessed based on the results of F1-score and F2-score, which ensure that identified samples are labeled as phishing with high confidence.
elements.sent-2411-566: We refer to the final output as PEEK phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-567: Unlike standard SOTA methods (e.g., Gmail and Outlook filters), our models focus on deeper contextual phishing detection, particularly targeting the email body discovery.
elements.sent-2411-568: To understand the diverse phishing strategies that emerge in PEEK phishing (RQ2), we start by applying an anomaly identification algorithm to identify samples that differ from both existing and PEEK phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-569: We refer to the PEEK phishing emails that are distributed within the majority phishing set but labeled as outliers as PEEK anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-570: These anomalies are then clustered into several interpretable categories for persuasive patterns analysis.
elements.sent-2411-571: Specifically, we used the isolation forest score (IFS) for anomaly identification.
elements.sent-2411-572: IFS is commonly applied in anomaly phishing detection and is generally robust to data noise \cite{liu2008isolation .}
elements.sent-2411-573: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is applied for text clustering, as it can automatically discover topics without requiring predefined categories~\cite{blei2003latent .}
elements.sent-2411-574: The optimal number of cluster topics is determined by the text coherence scores, which range from 0 to 1, with higher scores indicating stronger coherence.
elements.sent-2411-575: We then delve into the deceptive characteristics of each anomaly cluster, focusing on Cialdini's persuasion principles~\cite{cialdini2001science : Authority, Reciprocity, Scarcity, Liking, and Social Proof.}
elements.sent-2411-576: For instance, attackers might employ these tactics to create a sense of urgency and induce panic, thereby misleading victims into making irrational decisions.
elements.sent-2411-577: One of the goals of this study is to compare the persuasive tactics employed in traditional phishing with those found in LLM-generated phishing content.
elements.sent-2411-578: To annotate persuasion principles, we leverage LLOOM, an integrated tool that distills input data, clusters information, and outputs summarized concepts \cite{lam2024concept .}
elements.sent-2411-579: By providing the conceptual definitions of persuasion principles, LLooM performs inverse annotation to identify the principles within PEEK anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-580: To gain deeper insights into specific principles (e.g., differences in lexical usage), we employ Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count 2022 (LIWC-22) \cite{boyd2022development .}
elements.sent-2411-581: LIWC-22 quantifies linguistic features using a pre-compiled dictionary that maps words to psychological insights, encompassing emotional, social, cognitive, and authority-based language categories \cite{boyd2022development .}
elements.sent-2411-582: These insights align with psychologically meaningful word groups, each corresponding to one or more persuasion principles.
elements.sent-2411-583: For instance, the Social Proof principle reflects human tendencies toward conformity and peer influence, often indicated by terms like ``everyone'' and ``most people''.
elements.sent-2411-584: By leveraging these tools, we aim to 1) uncover deceptive characteristics through psychological and semantic analysis and 2) illustrate the evolution of phishing techniques, including variations in the use of persuasion principles, word choice, and emotional expression.
elements.subsection-2411-023: Loop-back Phishing Samples with New Patterns
elements.sent-2411-585: Our approach creates a recurrent iterative framework by reintegrating high-quality PEEK phishing samples into the training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-586: In this phase, we insert extracted features from Section \ref{chapter_pen_C (i.e., topic keywords, labels, and email content) into the chat prompt used during training, starting a new cycle of training, generation, and analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-587: This iterative loop enables the continuous refinement of the phishing sample dataset, integrating evolving patterns and themes while maintaining high-quality, diverse content generation that aligns with current phishing tactics.
elements.sent-2411-588: The goal of PEEK is to generate diverse phishing datasets that closely mimic human-like phishing attempts.
elements.sent-2411-589: To systematically evaluate the effectiveness of generated phishing emails, we introduce three primary evaluation criteria: Quality, Diversity, and Robustness.
elements.sent-2411-590: Quality assesses how closely the generated phishing emails resemble realistic phishing email campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-591: We evaluate quality from two aspects: the generator's performance and the realism validation of generated emails.
elements.sent-2411-592: To quantify the quality, we introduce the following metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-593: LLM Generation Coherence (LLM-GC): The metric measures the generative model's ability to produce contextually relevant outputs based solely on provided inputs (e.g., instructions, labels, keywords, and email prompts) without relying on external domain knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-594: The LLM-GC yields a coherence score ranging between 0 to 1 \cite{mimno2011optimizing , where scores closer to 1 reflect higher contextual consistency and relevance.}
elements.sent-2411-595: Perplexity (PPL): This metric quantifies the generative model's confidence in predicting subsequences in a given sequence, with values ranging from 1 upwards \cite{brown1992class .}
elements.sent-2411-596: Lower perplexity values (close to 1) indicate content closely mirroring the input data but potentially risking ease of classification, whereas higher perplexity suggest increased randomness in the generated content.
elements.sent-2411-597: Typically, perplexity scores between 1 and 10 strike a balance between authenticity and variety.
elements.sent-2411-598: Phishing Authentication Score (PAS): PAS evaluates the realism and authenticity of generated phishing content based on content structure, contextual relevance, and psychological manipulation tactics common in human-crafted phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-599: PAS is determined through a ChatGPT analyzer employing targeted prompts to classify samples, assign phishing authenticity scores, and provide explanatory rationales \cite{koide2024chatspamdetector .}
elements.sent-2411-600: Scores range from 0 to 10; higher scores indicate high-quality, authentic phishing imitations, and scores below 5 indicate poor realism or non-phishing characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-601: The ChatGPT analyzer's reliability was validated using 5-fold cross-validation tests, achieving F1 and F2 scores of 0.95 and 0.97, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-602: Detailed validation processes are provided in Appendix~\ref{appendix:analyzer_CV .}
elements.sent-2411-603: Diversity evaluates the novelty and variety of PEEK-generated phishing emails compared to existing datasets, focusing on thematic variance, deceptive strategies, and uniqueness of attack vectors.. Metrics include:.
elements.sent-2411-604: Isolation Forest Score (IFS): This metric identifies novel or outlier phishing emails based on negative isolation scores, derived by isolating content through randomly selected feature splits \cite{liu2008isolation .}
elements.sent-2411-605: Text content is numerically represented using Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) \cite{wu2008interpreting , highlighting distinctive linguistic features across datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-606: Deceptive Persuasion Score (DPS): DPS quantifies the semantic alignment of generated phishing emails with predefined persuasive principles (e.g., authority, urgency).

elements.sent-2411-646: To identify divergences in phishing topics, we employed Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) clustering to uncover latent thematic differences between PEEK phishing and existing phishing emails.
elements.sent-2411-647: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig_expanded_topic , the analysis indicates PEEK significantly expands the scope of phishing topics, facilitating richer and more varied phishing expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-648: Existing phishing email topics predominantly cover two main themes: 1) banking services related to account security, including online service feedback (T0), verification processes (T1), password updates (T2), and restricted account access (T4); and 2) business-oriented communications and extending company-related interactions (T3).
elements.sent-2411-649: While the majority of the PEEK phishing emails align closely with these established topics, LDA analysis reveals three additional emerging topics unique to PEEK.
elements.sent-2411-650: Specifically, topic T6 shifts away from typical banking themes toward shipment and delivery issues concerning contact address updates.
elements.sent-2411-651: These emails frequently employ misleading claims about incorrect addresses to facilitate direct interactions, such as prompting recipients to engage via phone calls (example in Fig.~\ref{fig_diversity_example1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-652: Furthermore, topics T8 and T9 introduce additional diversity by addressing bank account login failures and university online service interactions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-653: These findings demonstrate that PEEK can not only replicate existing phishing domains but also be triggered to diversify and innovate new phishing topics.
elements.sent-2411-654: The expanded phishing topics are given by open-ended keywords in crafted chat prompts, thus enabling flexible topic generation and enriching the simulated attacks landscape to enhance detector robustness against unseen threats.
elements.subsection-2411-025: RQ2: LLM-driven Phishing Patterns that Differ from Existing Phishing Datasets
elements.sent-2411-655: To address RQ2, which investigates whether and how LLM-generated phishing content differs from traditional phishing strategies in manipulating user perception, we applied the isolation forest (IFS) algorithm to identify subtle lexical outliers within a mixed dataset of existing phishing emails and LLM-generated phishing from the PEEK dataset.
elements.sent-2411-656: The analysis identified 945 PEEK samples as statistically distinct, hereafter referred to as PEEK anomalies ($PEEK_{anml}$) (Fig.~\ref{fig_iso_anomalies_normalies ).}
elements.sent-2411-657: To validate the lexical distinctiveness of these anomalies, we conducted a Mann-Whitney U test on IFS-selected features.
elements.sent-2411-658: Results confirmed significant differences between PEEK anomalies and normal samples (Fig.~\ref{fig_iso_split_features ), with lexical distinctions aligning with varied persuasive intentions.}
elements.sent-2411-659: These findings suggest that LLMs adopt alternative manipulation strategies that diverge from previously observed phishing tactics.

elements.sent-2411-660: To further analyze the persuasion complexity of PEEK anomalies, we computed the Deceptive Persuasion Score (DPS), which estimates the number and intensity of persuasive principles within each sample.
elements.sent-2411-661: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_general_principles_difference , PEEK anomalies consistently employ a wider array of principles with greater frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-662: Notably, 2.13\% of PEEK anomalies contain all six persuasion principles, and 40.9\% include five.
elements.sent-2411-663: In contrast, only 0.78\% of traditional phishing samples use all six persuasion principles, and 17.11\% employ five.
elements.sent-2411-664: This divergence is reflected in the usage patterns of key lexical elements.

elements.sent-2411-666: These results highlight the increased rhetorical complexity in LLM-generated content, underscoring the need to reevaluate detection methods.

elements.sent-2411-832: Generally speaking, for defending against adversarial attacks, detectors are trained by PEEK phishing to achieve the best performance, albeit with the performance of several models that are trained using non-PEEK phishing is better than that of PEEK phishing.
elements.sent-2411-833: Further PEEK against perturbative attacks are shown in Appendix~\ref{appendix:evasion_attacks_other_results , which adds a column of attack success rate to further indicate the obvious increase in evasion detection accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-834: The datasets provided by PEEK mitigate the attacks steadily and consistently, which is crucial for detectors.
elements.subsection-2411-030: PEEK phishing Defend Against Other Evasion Attacks
elements.sent-2411-835: As discussed in the main text, the inclusion of PEEK phishing in the training dataset significantly enhances the robustness of phishing detectors, particularly in maintaining high detection accuracy when subjected to adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-836: This improvement is evident across multiple evaluation metrics, including accuracy, F1-score, and adversarial evaluation metrics (EVA-Acc and EVA-F1), results shown in Tables \ref{tab:merged_performance_1 , \ref{tab:merged_performance_2 , and \ref{tab:merged_performance_3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-837: This section shows model performance under TextFooler, Pruthi, and PWWS before and after training with generated content.
elements.sent-2411-838: However, different models exhibit varying degrees of robustness when exposed to different types of adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-839: For instance, while RoBERTa-Syn consistently achieves high scores across multiple metrics, other models, such as T5-Encoder and DistilBERT, show more variable performance, suggesting that their ability to withstand adversarial perturbations depends on the nature of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-840: These observations highlight the importance of model selection in adversarial phishing detection.
elements.sent-2411-841: Aligning model choice with specific threat types encountered in real-world scenarios can improve detection reliability.
elements.sent-2411-842: Moreover, training with adversarially augmented datasets, such as those incorporating PEEK phishing, offers a promising strategy for enhancing model robustness.

elements.sent-2411-843: Here, we provide the training parameters for PEEK phishing models designed to defend against different types of attacks.
elements.sent-2411-844: We implemented two categories of phishing detectors.
elements.sent-2411-845: The first category, including Detectors ALBERT, RoBERTa, SQ~\cite{mehdi2023adversarial , are trained to distinguish between phishing emails and general emails.}
elements.sent-2411-846: The second category, comprising Detectors DistilBERT, RoBERTa-LLMs \cite{jamal2024improved and T5-Encoder \cite{bethany2024large aimed at differentiating between handwritten phishing emails and machine-generated phishing emails.}}
elements.sent-2411-847: This division allows for a more targeted defense strategy based on the nature of the phishing content.
elements.sent-2411-848: Such categorization also facilitates evaluating model robustness under different phishing generation paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-849: The performance of each model varies depending on the type of attack encountered.
elements.sent-2411-850: The specific models and their training parameters are summarized in Table \ref{tab:evasion_attack_model_parameters_1 and \ref{tab:evasion_attack_model_parameters_2 .}}

elements.section-2411-017: Persuasion Principle Usage Differences among Diverse Phishing Dominants.
elements.sent-2411-851: Fig.~\ref{fig_extra_principles_frequency_rest presents the distribution of persuasion principles utilized in phishing emails categorized under different themes, including banking, donations, job hiring, and shipment/package delivery.}
elements.sent-2411-852: The results indicate that phishing attacks employ distinct psychological manipulation strategies depending on the thematic context of the email.
elements.sent-2411-853: For instance, certain principles, such as Authority and Social Proof, may be more prevalent in banking-related phishing attempts, whereas Reciprocity and Scarcity might be more frequently observed in donation scams.
elements.sent-2411-854: Understanding these variations in attack strategies across different phishing themes is crucial, as it enables researchers and security practitioners to develop more effective countermeasures.
elements.sent-2411-855: Additionally, raising awareness among users about these persuasive tactics can help mitigate the risk of falling victim to phishing attempts, ultimately reducing potential financial and informational losses.
